content: This systematic review synthesizes findings from 17 sources to assess how damp heat aging affects the adhesion strength of polymeric encapsulants, with implications for DVS-PEI systems in flexible electronics. Damp heat exposure produces synergistic degradation: combined high temperature and humidity reduced adhesive joint tensile strength by 54.4%, substantially exceeding the 31.3% loss from moisture alone or 27.7% from thermal aging alone [1]. Adhesion energy (Gc) decreased across all tested encapsulant chemistries (EVA, POE, EPE) after 5,000 hours at 90 degrees C under both hot-dry and hot-humid conditions [2], with material ranking proving condition-dependent: POE demonstrated superior coupon-level thermal stability [9], while EVA showed less peel force degradation under damp heat at the module level [10]. Reducing vinyl acetate content to 24% improved tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking retention after 2,000 hours of damp heat aging [11]. XPS analysis confirmed hydrolytic degradation of silane coupling agent bonds at the encapsulant-glass interface [15], and the 2024 PVEL Scorecard reported DH failure rates doubling year-over-year, with 11% of tested configurations failing outright [17]. Quantitative adhesion data specific to DVS-PEI encapsulants under damp heat remain absent from the literature, representing the most critical evidence gap for flexible electronics applications. The IEC 61215 protocol (85 degrees C / 85% RH) provides a transferable framework for future DVS-PEI qualification studies.
